replies.text:
Economists call it creative destruction, but they're usually referring to zombie companies that are not good enough to grow or add much to the economy but not bad enough to die while times are good. They take up scarce capital and valuable talent that can't be used elsewhere. A small recession every now and then burns away these companies and frees up the money and people to be more productive elsewhere.

It seems the phenomenon is true in Hollywood as well as forest management.

replies.text: @nytu-p2n I saw the play and was vastly disappointed. The original broadway production received a LOT of hype, and there were many stories and previews showcasing the very talented performers “acting like cats.” That was exciting and they did a great job with that part of it. But the show had no plot from the beginning, just a bunch of nonsense vague and meaningless songs. The only hit was “Memories,” which was truly very beautiful. “Cats” and “Andrew Lloyd Weber” were enough to keep the hype going. I saw a touring company do the show and what a letdown. I had expected meaningful beautiful songs, a meaningful plot and, of course, lots of kitties. I was waiting for greatness, and it never arrived.

replies.author: @sarathered-frostfyre
replies.text:
I think if they'd just accepted that going with full on cat costumes was a bad idea, and that CGI was also a bad idea, and done funky street clothes and minimal makeup that would give "cat" they'd have been far better off. Or even dropping the cat altogether and having the only mention of cats be in the song lyrics themselves!

The best idea, if they had to do it, would have been to just do a fully animated production with cats that moved like cats, not cats dancing like humans! They should have gone with something like Aristocats or Oliver & Company, which worked (I know Oliver & Co. is not considered the classic Aristocats is, but it's still charming). Frankly, they should have just gone with it as a kids' film, period, and it would have worked better (so long as it was animated). Heck, even treating it as a variety show or a collection of dance/music scenes (a la Fantasia) would have been better. I still don't think it would have been a hit--not without anything resembling a plot--but it wouldn't have been THIS horror! 

And the very, very best idea would have been to not do it at all, heh.

replies.author: @justinklenk
replies.text:
​@ihatezgoogle6264
No, but it's a matter of standards.  After the initial inception of any technology, humans' appreciation for what the tool accomplishes begins to drop, precipitously. We shift our weight to the new/easier perspective, and lose the grounding we had when we appreciated what all was actually involved in an accomplishment.

So, for instance (with regard to VFX), in Jurassic Park, we were RIGHT in the middle of that transition - the sweet spot. When we saw that in the theater, there were ZERO questions or complaints or issues with the effects we'd just roller-coastered through - no details were experienced as (or even suspected of being) 'off'.  Mature, capable humans were in charge - as we would only ever expect.

replies.author: @justinklenk

author: @ceresta1732
text: The thing that ultimately really fucked the effects team was Hooper's refusal to use performance capture technology, which was designed explicitly to solve all of the problems this film had. No motion tracking suits, no additional cameras, absolutely nothing. Hooper had zero experience with CG direction, and famously refused to listen to any effects supervisor on the matter. Every single suit of CG fur had to be animated... By hand... And all they had to work with was whatever cameras we're filming that set. Fucking insane!

replies.author: @analogbunny
replies.text: ​@B-26354Half the team who wrote She-Ra (the original) were WWII vets, and all of them were in divisions that opened up Germany and West Poland. The show is mostly garbage, but they also have a few very good episodes about book burning, propaganda, state-enforced snitching, families being divided as some flee oppression and some stay to fight. author: @mikewazowski350
text: I worked for a company which went through a similar issue. They underbid all of the competition. When the production crew got wind of what they had to do, all the long hours it would take, and that most of them were on salary pay (not hourly), and not to forget that the bids included promises which were impossible, there was a rebellion. This went on for several years, bid after bid, until the company went bankrupt, but not before all the talent left for better jobs. I remember one meeting in the board room with management explaining what had to be done. The production manager was blank faced when they finished the presentation. Several of the techs and artists started to laugh and when asked, they said, "no fucking way, we can't do it". The management had no fucking clue what the company could actually do or the limits of the hardware we were working with and they refused to spend any money to upgrade.

replies.text: Eliot is an anagram of 'toilet', which is where Old Possum's Book belongs - it's short little verses that are great for reading one or two, while on the loo. A little nugget of whimsy while you push out little nuggets of the opposite of whimsy.

replies.author: @hariman7727
replies.text:
​@sihollett...
Congratulations, you win the award for "Most insultingly insipid waste of time comment" today. 

Please collect your prize by going to the corner and hanging your head in shame.  You are also now assigned the task of collecting a set of crutches for that joke.

replies.author: @smorris12
replies.text: ​@sihollettYou know how anagrams work, don't you?.....

replies.author: @sihollett
replies.text: @smorris12 sorry, yes you need the T and S to make toilets.

replies.author: @smorris12
replies.text: ​@sihollettChrist, if this argument was any more shored up there'd be a bulldozer on site.

replies.author: @sihollett
replies.text:
​@hariman7727 Old Possum's is meant to be (in its published form, rather than its original form of personal poems to specific people) a perfect sort of book for sitting on the loo - if you are a snob you might baulk and tell people to hang their head in shame, but that's a high honour as it means that it's
 - something to enjoy while doing something that's not particularly enjoyable
 - something to reflect on while you have reflection time
 - something you dip into often rather than leave in a library

I wasn't joking about it being a book that belongs in the toilet. Eliot is a far better short-form writer than I, but I was trying to (in a short form so as to try and not waste people's time) make a fundamental point about why people 'forget' Old Possum's when thinking about Cats - they are fundamentally different things.

Old Possum's a collection that published what were individual poems sent personally to children. It's meant to be read for a couple of minutes and savoured, not guzzled down in one go - which is what you get with Cats.

As an apparently award-winning creator of 'insultingly insipid' content I would suggest that Cats is an insultingly insipid adaptation that doesn't understand the basic nature of the source material. It's a couple-of-minutes-at-a-time set of individual short poems, not a two-plus-hours-in-one-go single work. This is why there was an insistence on no plot or story - to highlight the nature of the original as not being one unified work, but many disperse little bits on a theme.

replies.author: @hariman7727
replies.text:
​@sihollettI'm impressed by your ability to claim that being a jerk is something to be proud of. 

Cats is designed to be an entertaining visual spectacle with an overarching story underneath if you are willing to look. 

I never said it was perfect. 

I never said it was some special life-changing thing. 

You're the one who's posturing and flapping around about how the book is only worth reading in short little tidbits, and TS Eliot is awful... Because you say so. 

Congratulations, you have won the "proud of your own farts" award.

replies.text:
At what point does the source material take the blame for the movie adaptation? This movie takes everything wrong with the stage play and adds a whole new layer of suckage with miscast stunt-cast actors (which is a stretch when Taylor Swift is among the cast) clad in fursuits and covered in freakish looking CGI. I don't care if it ran nearly 20 years on Broadway, that still doesn't mean it was any good. Of all the musicals Hollywood could have chosen to make a movie out of, they chose that one. Name recognition was the only reason for that.

How bad do you have to be to make *The Movie: A Chorus Line* almost look good by comparison? That's a better example of something that worked better on the stage than on the screen, and that was 40 years ago! At least that show had better songs to work with.

author: @e.m.dubois5463
text:
One time I'll kinda push back on Drinker, and of films, it had to be fucking CATS, lol.

The reason why heavy make-up and costumes works on stage isn't because people don't take stage seriously. There's a reason why if a stage play hits Broadways, it stands a serious chance of having a movie made of it, or why so many stars are found there. Stage is taken very, very seriously.

The reason why putting people in suits and makeup works is proximity. In movies, we expect cameras to get up-close and personal with the performers, showing us every detail of the costume, makeup, and the CGI that'll age with every new higher-resolution screen they're put on. 

But on stage, we're sitting pretty far away even in the front rows, and our eyes can only take in so much at a time. You can't make out the little corners cut or mistakes made as easily. It doesn't mean you don't take it seriously, you just can't pause and go back a few frames to check if you really saw a zipper, unlike with a movie like Cats, where you can pause, go back a bit, and check to see if someone's fur CGI fur clipped through their costume, lol.
